title: Home Is Where the School Is: The Logic of Homeschooling and the Emotional Labor of Mothering By Jennifer Lois New York University Press. 2013. 239 pages. $22 paper
abstract: Jennifer Lois has written an engaging book about homeschooling mothers that seeks to dispel the myriad myths surrounding them. Lois covers the whys and hows of homeschooling, the domestic and emotional labor and potential burnout, the justifications and expectations, and how all of this is accomplished within the social construction of the “good mother.” Lois's methodology is thorough. For this work she conducted extensive field research that included one-on-one interviews with 24 mothers and follow-up interviews with 16 of them, six years later. In addition, she attended meetings of the Parents' Association for Teaching at Home (PATH) in her community. Because of her thorough research, we have a systematic ethnographic study that exposes the taken-for-granted assumptions among homeschooling moms and the rituals that guide them. The homeschooling moms with whom Lois spoke were, perhaps, what you would expect them to be. Most were middle class, and of the original 24, two were Hispanic American and one was African American. Additionally, 21 of the 24 identified as religious (14 of those identified as highly conservative and evangelical Christians). With these kinds of numbers, we would expect a chapter to deal specifically with the issue of faith and homeschooling. While Lois intersperses comments that the moms make regarding religion, there is no in-depth analysis of it. In fact, I expected “religion” to appear in the index, given how central it was to almost all of the women. In her defense, Lois discusses her discomfort discussing faith with the mothers since she herself has limited knowledge of the Bible. However, this religious angle is perhaps what we most “assume” when it comes to mothers (or families) that choose homeschooling, so more analysis of its role seems worthy.
